summary: Patrick McKenzie and Ramit Sethi discuss practical strategies for acquiring first consulting clients, emphasizing that most business is won through relationships and research long before any formal pitch. They argue that engineers systematically undercharge and fail to communicate in terms clients care about — business outcomes rather than technical details. The conversation covers how deep customer research reveals the exact language and pain points that make sales inevitable, why charging too little destroys credibility, and how to position yourself as someone who understands the client's business at a visceral level. They stress that the gap between commodity freelancing and premium consulting is primarily about repositioning and communication, not technical skill.
key_points: 85% of winning a consulting engagement happens before you sit down in the room — through relationships, research, and positioning
Radically undercharging for early work destroys both income and credibility; $10/hour signals you're not worth taking seriously at the CEO level
Engineers fail at selling because they talk about technology instead of business outcomes — no client wakes up thinking they have a WordPress problem, they have a business problem
Deep customer research using their exact words is what separates premium offerings from commodity ones, and statistical significance is irrelevant in qualitative customer research
If everyone in an industry does something a particular way, the radical notion is that they're not all simultaneously making the same mistake — move from disparagement to curiosity
money_quotes: 85 percent of the work is done before you ever sit down in the room.
$10 is not a meaningful amount of money to any business.
No SEO wakes up in the morning and thinks, 'Damn, I have a WordPress problem.' They wake up in the morning and say, 'Damn, I have a business problem.'
Nothing motivates people like having their own words repeated right back to them.
Statistical significance is irrelevant when you're doing customer research.
If you can't sketch someone's business model on a napkin, I don't think you can ever sell them anything worthwhile.
I just don't give a damn about technology. I really don't. What I care about is, is my business going to generate revenue?
Focus on taking your B+ customers/prospects to be A customers. Don't focus on the D people, trying to turn them into A customers. It will never happen.
key_insight: Premium consulting is sold by demonstrating deep understanding of the client's business problems in their own words, not by showcasing technical skills or competing on price.
